nodes.name: Olympics
nodes.description: Olympic Games history, host cities, and notable events

items.prompt: Which city hosted the first modern Summer Olympics in 1896?
items.explanation:
1896 Summer Olympics, Greece
First modern Olympic Games, revived by Pierre de Coubertin and the International Olympic Committee
241 athletes from 14 nations competed in 43 events
Held at the Panathenaic Stadium, a marble stadium rebuilt from ancient ruins
No gold medals were awarded—winners received silver medals and olive branches
The marathon event was inspired by the legend of Pheidippides running from Marathon to Athens
Greek water carrier Spyridon Louis won the marathon, becoming a national hero

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1908 Summer Olympics, originally awarded to Rome before Italy withdrew due to the eruption of Mount Vesuvius?
items.explanation:
1908 Summer Olympics, United Kingdom
Originally awarded to Rome, but Italy withdrew after the 1906 eruption of Mount Vesuvius diverted funds
First Olympics to feature a purpose-built stadium (White City Stadium, 70,000 capacity)
The marathon distance was set at 26 miles 385 yards to finish in front of the Royal Box—this became the standard marathon distance in 1921
Italian runner Dorando Pietri was disqualified from the marathon after officials helped him across the finish line
22 sports and 110 events with 2,008 athletes from 22 nations
First Olympics where athletes marched behind national flags in the opening ceremony
items.cardFront: 1908, originally awarded to Rome, set marathon distance

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1936 Summer Olympics, controversially under Nazi Germany?
items.explanation:
1936 Summer Olympics, Nazi Germany
Hitler intended the Games as a showcase for Aryan racial superiority
Jesse Owens, a Black American, won four gold medals (100m, 200m, long jump, 4x100m relay)—undermining Nazi ideology
First Olympics to be televised, reaching viewers in Berlin and Potsdam
Leni Riefenstahl's film Olympia pioneered sports cinematography techniques still used today
Several nations debated boycotting due to Nazi persecution of Jews; most competed anyway
49 nations participated with 3,963 athletes—the largest Games to that point
items.cardFront: 1936, Nazi Germany, Jesse Owens won 4 golds

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1948 Summer Olympics, nicknamed the 'Austerity Games' because they were held in the aftermath of World War II?
items.explanation:
1948 Summer Olympics, United Kingdom
Nicknamed the "Austerity Games"—held amid postwar rationing, with no new venues built
First Games held after a 12-year gap (1936)—no Olympics during WWII
Germany and Japan were banned from competing
Fanny Blankers-Koen of the Netherlands won four gold medals in track and field, nicknamed the "Flying Housewife"
First Games shown on home television in the UK, though few owned sets
4,104 athletes from 59 nations competed at Wembley Stadium
The 1940 Games had been awarded to Tokyo, then Helsinki, before being cancelled

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1964 Summer Olympics, the first ever held in Asia?
items.explanation:
1964 Summer Olympics, Japan
First Olympic Games held in Asia
Showcased Japan's postwar economic recovery and technological advancement
Introduced the Shinkansen (bullet train), which began service just before the Games opened
Satellite technology enabled live international television broadcast for the first time
Judo debuted as an Olympic sport—in the host country where it originated
The Olympic flame was lit by Yoshinori Sakai, born in Hiroshima on August 6, 1945—the day the atomic bomb was dropped
5,151 athletes from 93 nations participated
items.cardFront: 1964, first Olympics in Asia, bullet train debut

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1968 Summer Olympics, remembered for the Black Power salute on the medal podium and Bob Beamon's record long jump?
items.explanation:
1968 Summer Olympics, Mexico
First Olympics held in Latin America and at high altitude (2,240m / 7,350ft)
Tommie Smith and John Carlos raised black-gloved fists on the 200m podium in the iconic Black Power salute
Bob Beamon shattered the long jump record by nearly 2 feet (8.90m)—it stood for 23 years
Dick Fosbury debuted the "Fosbury Flop" high jump technique, which became universal
Ten days before the Games, Mexican military killed hundreds of student protesters in the Tlatelolco massacre
The high altitude aided sprinters and jumpers but challenged distance runners
5,516 athletes from 112 nations competed
items.cardFront: 1968, Black Power salute, Bob Beamon's long jump

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1972 Summer Olympics, tragically marred by the terrorist attack in which 11 Israeli athletes and coaches were killed?
items.explanation:
1972 Summer Olympics, West Germany
Palestinian militant group Black September took 11 Israeli team members hostage; all were killed
The Games were suspended for 34 hours—IOC President Avery Brundage controversially declared "The Games must go on"
Mark Spitz won seven gold medals in swimming, all in world-record times
Soviet basketball team controversially defeated the U.S. in the gold-medal game after officials added extra time
Designed as the "Cheerful Games" to contrast with Berlin 1936—security was intentionally minimal
7,134 athletes from 121 nations participated
The attack led to the creation of counter-terrorism units worldwide, including Germany's GSG 9

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1976 Summer Olympics, notorious for massive cost overruns that left the city in debt for 30 years?
items.explanation:
1976 Summer Olympics, Canada
Original budget of $124 million ballooned to over $1.5 billion—the city did not pay off the debt until 2006
The Olympic Stadium's retractable roof was never completed in time; nicknamed "The Big O" (also "The Big Owe")
29 African nations boycotted over New Zealand's rugby ties with apartheid South Africa
14-year-old Romanian gymnast Nadia Comaneci scored the first perfect 10.0 in Olympic gymnastics history
The scoreboard could not display 10.00 and showed 1.00 instead—Comaneci earned seven perfect scores total
6,084 athletes from 92 nations competed (reduced by the African boycott)

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1984 Summer Olympics, boycotted by the Soviet bloc, and became the first Games to turn a major profit through corporate sponsorship?
items.explanation:
1984 Summer Olympics, United States
Soviet Union and 13 allied nations boycotted in retaliation for the U.S. boycott of Moscow 1980
First Olympics to generate a significant profit (~$215 million) through private funding and corporate sponsorship
No new permanent venues built—used existing infrastructure like the LA Memorial Coliseum
Carl Lewis matched Jesse Owens by winning four track and field golds (100m, 200m, long jump, 4x100m)
Mary Lou Retton became the first American woman to win gymnastics all-around gold
Only city to bid for the 1984 Games after Montreal's financial disaster scared others away
The model of corporate-funded Games became standard for future Olympics

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1988 Summer Olympics, marking South Korea's emergence on the world stage and remembered for Ben Johnson's steroid disqualification?
items.explanation:
1988 Summer Olympics, South Korea
First Olympics without a major boycott since 1972—159 nations participated
Ben Johnson won the 100m final in 9.79s but was stripped of gold after testing positive for stanozolol
Florence Griffith-Joyner ("Flo-Jo") set still-standing world records in the 100m (10.49s) and 200m (21.34s)
Greg Louganis hit his head on the springboard during diving preliminaries, then won gold
South Korea used the Games to showcase its transformation from war-torn nation to economic power
Tennis returned as a full medal sport for the first time since 1924
North Korea boycotted after being denied co-host status

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1992 Summer Olympics, the first Games with no boycotts since 1972 and featuring the U.S. basketball 'Dream Team'?
items.explanation:
1992 Summer Olympics, Spain
First Games since 1972 with no boycotts—169 nations participated, including a post-Soviet Unified Team
The U.S. "Dream Team" (Jordan, Magic, Bird, et al.) dominated basketball, winning by an average of 44 points
First Games where professional athletes competed openly (NBA players in basketball)
The opening ceremony featured an archer lighting the cauldron by shooting a flaming arrow over it
South Africa returned after 32 years of apartheid-era bans
Germany competed as a unified team for the first time since 1964
9,356 athletes from 169 nations—the largest Games to date at that time
items.cardFront: 1992, Dream Team, no boycotts, post-Cold War Games

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 1996 Summer Olympics, marking the centennial of the modern Games, marred by the Centennial Park bombing?
items.explanation:
1996 Summer Olympics, United States
Centennial Olympic Games—100 years after Athens 1896
A pipe bomb exploded in Centennial Olympic Park, killing 2 and injuring 111
Security guard Richard Jewell discovered the bomb but was wrongly suspected; Eric Rudolph was convicted in 2003
Muhammad Ali, visibly affected by Parkinson's, lit the Olympic cauldron—one of the most emotional moments in Games history
Michael Johnson won gold in both the 200m and 400m, wearing his signature gold shoes
10,318 athletes from 197 nations—a record at the time
Controversially awarded over Athens, the sentimental favorite for the centennial

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 2000 Summer Olympics, often called the 'best Olympics ever' by IOC president Juan Antonio Samaranch?
items.explanation:
2000 Summer Olympics, Australia
IOC President Juan Antonio Samaranch called them "the best Olympic Games ever"
Cathy Freeman, an Indigenous Australian, won the 400m gold—one of the most celebrated moments in Australian sports
Freeman lit the Olympic cauldron at the opening ceremony, symbolizing reconciliation
First Games to feature the triathlon and taekwondo as medal sports
Ian Thorpe ("Thorpedo") won three swimming golds for Australia at age 17
10,651 athletes from 199 nations competed
The Sydney Opera House and Harbour Bridge provided iconic backdrops for sailing and marathon events

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 2008 Summer Olympics, featuring the 'Bird's Nest' stadium and Michael Phelps winning a record eight gold medals?
items.explanation:
2008 Summer Olympics, China
Michael Phelps won 8 gold medals—the most by any athlete at a single Games
The National Stadium ("Bird's Nest"), designed by Herzog & de Meuron and Ai Weiwei, became an architectural icon
Usain Bolt burst onto the scene, winning 100m (world record 9.69s) and 200m (world record 19.30s)
Opening ceremony directed by Zhang Yimou was the most elaborate in Olympic history, with 15,000 performers
10,942 athletes from 204 nations—the most countries ever at that time
Controversies included air quality concerns, human rights criticism, and a lip-syncing scandal at the opening
China topped the gold medal count (51) for the first time, surpassing the U.S. (36)
items.cardFront: 2008, Bird's Nest, Phelps 8 golds, Bolt's debut

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 2012 Summer Olympics, becoming the first city to host the Games three times?
items.explanation:
2012 Summer Olympics, United Kingdom
London became the first city to host three Summer Olympics (1908, 1948, 2012)
Usain Bolt repeated his Beijing double, winning the 100m and 200m again—first to defend both sprint titles
Mo Farah won the 5,000m and 10,000m double for Great Britain on "Super Saturday"
Opening ceremony directed by Danny Boyle celebrated British culture from Shakespeare to the NHS to Mr. Bean
Michael Phelps became the most decorated Olympian ever with 22 medals (18 gold at that point)
10,568 athletes from 204 nations competed
The Olympic Park in Stratford regenerated a formerly industrial area of East London
items.cardFront: 2012, first city to host three times, Bolt double repeat

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 2016 Summer Olympics, the first ever held in South America?
items.explanation:
2016 Summer Olympics, Brazil
First Olympic Games held in South America
Usain Bolt completed an unprecedented "triple triple"—three consecutive 100m, 200m, and 4x100m golds (2008–2016)
Michael Phelps competed in his fifth Olympics, finishing with 23 gold medals—a record unlikely to be broken
Golf returned to the Olympics for the first time since 1904; rugby sevens debuted
Organizational challenges included the Zika virus outbreak, polluted waterways, and budget overruns
Host nation Brazil won its first Olympic gold in men's football, with Neymar scoring the winning penalty
11,238 athletes from 207 nations competed
items.cardFront: 2016, first in South America, Bolt's triple triple

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 2020 Summer Olympics, postponed to 2021 due to the COVID-19 pandemic and held largely without spectators?
items.explanation:
2020 Summer Olympics (held 2021), Japan
First Olympics postponed (not cancelled) in peacetime—delayed one year by COVID-19
Held almost entirely without spectators due to pandemic restrictions
Retained the name "Tokyo 2020" despite being held in 2021
Simone Biles withdrew from multiple events citing the "twisties" (a dangerous mental block), sparking global conversation about athlete mental health
New sports included skateboarding, surfing, sport climbing, and karate
Caleb Dressel (U.S.) won 5 swimming golds; Elaine Thompson-Herah (Jamaica) repeated as 100m/200m champion
11,420 athletes from 206 nations competed

items.prompt: Which city hosted the 2024 Summer Olympics, its first since 1924, with iconic events held at landmarks like the Eiffel Tower and Palace of Versailles?
items.explanation:
2024 Summer Olympics, France
Paris's third time hosting (1900, 1924, 2024)—exactly 100 years after its last Games
Opening ceremony held on the Seine River with athletes on boats—first ceremony outside a stadium
Events staged at landmarks: beach volleyball at the Eiffel Tower, equestrian at Versailles, fencing at the Grand Palais
Breaking (breakdancing) debuted as an Olympic sport
Leon Marchand became a French national hero with 4 swimming golds
The U.S. led the overall medal count; China and the U.S. tied with 40 golds each
10,714 athletes from 206 nations competed
Los Angeles hosts next in 2028, followed by Brisbane in 2032
items.cardFront: 2024, 100 years since Paris 1924, Seine opening ceremony
